questions.text: Wow! He just won a gold medal!
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Imperative sentences give commands or requests. This sentence does not instruct anyone to do something, so it cannot be classified as imperative.
Interrogative sentences ask questions, typically starting with words like 'who', 'what', 'where', 'when', or 'why'. This sentence is not asking anything; thus, it is not interrogative.

Strong feelings or excitement are expressed in exclamatory sentences. Exclamation points are frequently used by them! This sentence is the best choice because it uses the exclamation point and "Wow!" to express excitement.
Optative sentences express wishes or hopes, usually starting with phrases like 'May' or 'I wish.' This sentence does not express a wish, so it is not optative.

questions.explanation: Exclamatory sentences express strong emotions or excitement. They often use exclamation marks! This sentence clearly conveys excitement with "Wow!" and the exclamation mark, making it the most fitting option.

questions.text: Whatever you do, I will support you.
questions.subject: Scientific Reasoning

options.explanation: A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause with no dependent clauses. The sentence 'Whatever you do, I will support you' includes dependent and independent clauses, so this option is incorrect.

A compound sentence contains two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. Since 'Whatever you do, I will support you' has a dependent clause, it does not qualify as a compound sentence, making this option incorrect.

One independent clause and at least one dependent clause make up a complex sentence. The independent clause "I will support you" and the dependent clause "Whatever you do" make up the sentence "Whatever you do, I will support you."

A compound-complex sentence has at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. Since 'Whatever you do, I will support you' contains only one independent clause, indicating it does not meet the criteria for a compound-complex sentence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. The sentence "Whatever you do, I will support you" has the independent clause "I will support you" and the dependent clause "Whatever you do."

questions.text: I passed the test, but I would have gotten a perfect score if I had studied for their vocabulary section.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause. This option is incorrect because the given sentence has multiple clauses.

A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. This option is partially correct but does not account for the dependent clause present in the sentence.
A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. This option is incorrect because the given sentence has two independent clauses.

The sentence consists of one dependent clause ("if I had studied for their vocabulary section") and two independent clauses ("I passed the test" and "I would have gotten a perfect score"). It is a compound-complex sentence as a result.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses (“I passed the test” and “I would have gotten a perfect score”) and one dependent clause (“if I had studied for their vocabulary section”). Therefore, it is a compound-complex sentence.

questions.text: Please shut the door to keep out the bugs.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A command, request, or instruction is expressed in an imperative sentence. "Please shut the door" in this instance instructs someone to do just that.
An interrogative sentence asks a question and typically begins with words like "who," "what," "where," "when," "why," or "how." This sentence does not pose a question.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion or excitement, often ending with an exclamation mark. This sentence does not convey an emotion or exclamation.
An optative sentence expresses a wish or desire. While 'Please shut the door' is polite, it does not convey a wish but rather a command.

questions.explanation: An imperative sentence expresses a command, request, or instruction. In this case, "Please shut the door" directly tells someone to close the door.

questions.text: He succeeded, although his success was not expected.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause and no subordinate clauses, which is not the case here.
A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. This sentence has a subordinate clause, making this option incorrect.

The sentence consists of one subordinate clause (although his success was unexpected) and one main clause (He succeeded). It is a complex sentence because it contains both a main and a subordinate clause.
A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and one or more subordinate clauses. This sentence has only one independent clause, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence has one main clause (He succeeded) and one subordinate clause (although his success was not expected). Since it has a main and a subordinate clause, it is a complex sentence.

questions.text: My friend is now in Multan, and I shall go there to meet him.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause. The given sentence has two clauses, so this option is incorrect.
"My friend is now in Multan" and "I shall go there to meet him" are two independent clauses that are joined by the coordinating conjunction "and," making the sentence a compound.
A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. Since the given sentence has two independent clauses, this option is incorrect.
A compound-complex sentence has at least two independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. The given sentence does not contain any dependent clauses, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses, “My friend is now in Multan” and “I shall go there to meet him,” joined by the coordinating conjunction "and," so it is a compound sentence.

questions.text: He was scared to lose me.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A subject complement follows a linking verb and renames or describes the subject. While 'scared' describes 'he,' it does not rename him, so it is not a subject complement.
An object complement provides information about the object of a sentence. In this case, there is no direct object related to 'scared,' making this option incorrect.

A verb complement usually completes the meaning of the verb. Although 'scared' relates to the verb 'was,' it specifically describes the subject rather than completing the verb's meaning; thus, this is not the right choice.
An adjective complement describes the subject and comes after a linking verb. In this instance, "scared" is an adjective complement since it accurately characterizes "he's" emotional state.

questions.explanation: An adjective complement follows a linking verb and describes the subject. Here, "scared" clearly describes the emotional state of "he", making it an adjective complement.

questions.text: While Ali reads novels, Zia reads comics, but Hassan reads only fairy tales.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause and no dependent clauses. This sentence has multiple clauses, so it is not a simple sentence.

A compound sentence consists of at least two independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. While the sentence contains multiple independent clauses, it also includes a dependent clause, making it more complex.
A complex sentence has one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. This sentence has two independent clauses and one dependent clause, so it does not qualify as a complex sentence.

One independent clause (Zia reads comic books) is joined with the dependent clause (While Ali reads the novels), and another independent clause (Hassan reads only fairy tales) is joined with "but". This indicates that the sentence is compound-complex since it contains two independent clauses and one dependent clause.

questions.explanation: The dependent clause (While Ali reads the novels) is attached to one independent clause (Zia reads comics), and another independent clause (Hassan reads only fairy tales) is joined with "but". This means the sentence has two independent clauses and one dependent clause, which makes it a compound-complex sentence.

questions.text: Ali does not like novels since they are lengthy, so he does not read them.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause with no dependent clauses. This option is incorrect because the sentence contains multiple clauses.
A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by a conjunction. This option is incorrect as the sentence also includes a dependent clause.
A complex sentence consists of one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. This option is incorrect because the sentence contains two independent clauses, making it more complex.

The sentence consists of one dependent clause ("since they are lengthy") and two independent clauses ("Ali does not like novels" and "he does not read them"). It is a compound-complex statement as a result.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses (“Ali does not like novels” and “he does not read them”) and one dependent clause (“since they are lengthy”). Therefore, it is a compound-complex sentence.

questions.text: He must work very hard, or he will not win the first prize.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence consists of a single independent clause with no dependent clauses. This is not the case here, as the sentence contains two independent clauses.

The coordinating conjunction "or" connects the sentence's two independent clauses, "He must work very hard" and "He will not win the first prize." It is a compound sentence because it has two independent clauses and no dependent clauses.
A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. This sentence does not have any dependent clauses, making this option incorrect.
A compound-complex sentence includes at least two independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. Since there are no dependent clauses in this sentence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses (“He must work very hard” and “he will not win the first prize”) joined by the coordinating conjunction 'or'. Since it contains two independent clauses and no dependent clauses, it is a compound sentence.

questions.text: Your friends want to come to my party.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A subject complement follows a linking verb and describes the subject. In this sentence, 'to my party' does not describe 'friends' but rather indicates what they desire.
By illustrating what your friends desire, the phrase "to my party" completes the meaning of the verb "want," making it an object complement.
An adverb complement modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb. In this sentence, 'to my party' does not modify a verb or adverb.
An adjective complement typically follows an adjective and adds to its meaning. 'To my party' does not function in this way in the sentence.

questions.explanation: The word “to my party” completes the meaning of the verb “want” by showing what your friends want, so it is an object complement.

questions.text: Ali bought a novel, and he finished it in three days.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause. The given sentence has two independent clauses, so this option is incorrect.
The sentence is a compound sentence because the conjunction "and" joins two independent clauses (Ali bought a novel, and he finished it in three days).
A complex sentence consists of one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. The given sentence does not have any dependent clauses, so this option is incorrect.
A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. The given sentence does not have a dependent clause, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses (Ali bought a novel, and he finished it in three days) joined by the conjunction “and”, making it a compound sentence.

questions.text: She runs every day, so she is very fit.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause with no coordinating conjunctions. The given sentence has two independent clauses, so this option is incorrect.

Two or more independent clauses joined by semicolons or coordinating conjunctions (like "and") make up a compound sentence. Two independent clauses are joined by "so" in the sentence. The sentence is compound.
A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. The given sentence does not have any dependent clauses, so this option is incorrect.

A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. The given sentence has only two independent clauses and no dependent clauses; thus, it is not compound-complex.

questions.explanation: A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses connected by coordinating conjunctions (such as "and") or semicolons. The sentence contains two independent clauses connected by "so". It's a compound sentence.

questions.text: Can you call on his cell phone?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion. The sentence 'Can you call on his cell phone? ' does not make a statement; therefore, it is not a declarative sentence.
An imperative sentence gives a command or request. The sentence 'Can you call on his cell phone? ' is not issuing a command; rather, it is seeking information. Thus, it is not an imperative sentence.
"Can you call on his cell phone?" is a question that inquires as to whether calling someone's cell phone is feasible. As a result, it is an interrogative sentence, which poses a query.

An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion or excitement. The sentence 'Can you call on his cell phone? ' does not convey excitement; it is a straightforward question. Therefore, it is not an exclamatory sentence.

questions.explanation: The sentence "Can you call on his cell phone?" is a question, and it asks about the ability or possibility of calling someone's cell phone. It is therefore an interrogative sentence, which is a type of sentence that asks a question.

questions.text: Everybody clapped at the performance enthusiastically.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A situation or a fact is stated in the sentence. The sentence makes a claim and provides information. It is a declarative sentence because it makes no wishes or commands.
This type of sentence gives a command or instruction. Since the given sentence does not direct someone to do something, it cannot be classified as imperative.
This type of sentence expresses a wish or desire. The provided sentence does not convey any wishes, so it does not fit this category.
This sentence type expresses strong emotion or excitement, often featuring an exclamation mark. The provided sentence is a statement rather than an expression of emotion, so it is not exclamatory.

questions.explanation: The sentence states a fact or describes a situation. The sentence gives information and makes a statement. Since it does not give a command or wish, it’s a declarative sentence.

questions.text: What lovely weather you all are enjoying!
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This type of sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion. The given sentence does not provide information or assert a fact.
This type of sentence issues a command or request. The sentence in question does not instruct anyone to do anything.
This type expresses a wish or desire. While the sentence expresses a positive feeling about the weather, it does not convey a wish.

The sentence uses the word "lovely" and the exclamatory phrase "what...!" to convey strong feelings and admiration for the weather. It is an expression of excitement and joy rather than a declaration, order, or wish.

questions.explanation: The sentence expresses strong emotions and admiration for the weather, using the word "lovely" and the exclamatory phrase "what...!". It's not a statement, command, or wish, but an expression of enthusiasm and delight.

questions.text: Do you have any pencil?
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence states a fact or opinion and ends with a period. It does not ask a question.
An imperative sentence gives a command or request. It often starts with a verb and can end with a period or exclamation mark. This sentence does not command.
"Do you have any pencil?" is a question. It is therefore an interrogative sentence.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion and ends with an exclamation mark. This sentence does not convey strong feelings.

questions.explanation: The sentence “Do you have any pencil?” is asking a question. That makes it an interrogative sentence.

questions.text: Without a doubt, may he get the first position!
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This type of sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion. The given sentence does not merely state a fact, so it is not declarative.
Imperative sentences give commands or requests. The sentence in question does not issue a command but rather expresses a wish, making it not imperative.

The phrase conveys a wish or desire, namely the hope that someone will be given the top spot. The phrase "may he" is a feature of optative sentences, which convey a desire or wish for something or someone.
Exclamatory sentences express strong emotions or feelings, often ending with an exclamation mark. The sentence does not convey strong emotion but rather a wish, so it is not exclamatory.

questions.explanation: The sentence expresses a wish or desire, specifically hoping that someone gets the first position. The phrase "may he" is a characteristic of optative sentences, which express a wish or desire for someone or something.

questions.text: Wish you a happy journey together.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence states a fact or opinion. It typically ends with a period, and the structure of the sentence does not indicate a wish or desire. Example: "She is going on a journey."
An imperative sentence gives a command or request. It often begins with a verb and can end with a period or an exclamation mark. Example: "Go on your journey."

The sentence expresses a wish or desire, specifically hoping that someone has a happy journey together. The phrase "wish you" is a characteristic of optative sentences, which express a desire or wish for someone or something.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion and typically ends with an exclamation mark. Example: "What a wonderful journey!" This sentence does not convey strong emotion but rather a hope.

questions.explanation: The sentence expresses a wish or desire, specifically hoping that someone has a happy journey together. The phrase "wish you" is a characteristic of optative sentences, which express a desire or wish for someone or something.

questions.text: God showers his blessings.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Just a fact is stated in the sentence. It doesn't give an order or request (imperative), express a wish (optative), or convey abrupt, intense emotions (exclamatory).
This type of sentence gives a command or request. 'God showers his blessings' does not instruct anyone to do something, so this option is incorrect.
Optative sentences express wishes or desires. Since the sentence here states a fact about blessings, this option is not applicable.
Exclamatory sentences express strong emotions or feelings. The sentence provided does not convey any strong feelings, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence is simply stating a fact. It does not express a wish (optative), give a command/request (imperative), or show sudden, strong feelings (exclamatory).

questions.text: Please stop!
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion. It typically ends with a period and is not intended to give a command or request.

"Please stop!" is an imperative sentence that directs the listener to cease their current activity. Imperative sentences frequently start with a verb and occasionally add words like "please" to make the directive seem more courteous.
An optative sentence expresses a desire or wish. It is not applicable here, as "Please stop!" does not express a wish but rather gives a command.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion or excitement and usually ends with an exclamation mark. "Please stop!" is not expressing emotion but issuing a command.

questions.explanation: The sentence "Please stop!" is an imperative sentence, giving a direct command or instruction to the listener, telling them to stop what they are doing. Imperative sentences often begin with a verb and sometimes include words like "please" to make the command more polite.

questions.text: She asked whether I liked her dress.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Despite reporting a question, the sentence itself is declarative, stating that she inquired about your thoughts on her outfit.
An imperative sentence gives a command or request. 'She asked whether I liked her dress' does not instruct anyone to do anything.
Optative sentences express wishes. This sentence does not convey a wish but rather reports a question asked.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion or excitement. The sentence in question does not convey such emotion.

questions.explanation: Although the sentence reports a question, the sentence itself is a declarative sentence, stating a fact that she asked about your opinion on her dress.

questions.text: If you study hard, you will pass.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause and no dependent clauses. This option is incorrect because the sentence has a dependent clause.
A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. This option is incorrect because the sentence contains a dependent clause.
It is a complex sentence because it has one independent clause (you will pass) and one dependent clause (if you study hard).
A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and at least one dependent clause. This option is incorrect because there is only one independent clause in the given sentence.

questions.explanation: One independent clause (you will pass) and one dependent clause (if you study hard) make it a complex sentence.

questions.text: It had rained for days; the town was flooded.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence consists of just one independent clause. This sentence contains two independent clauses, so it cannot be classified as simple.
The two independent clauses in the sentence, "It had rained for days" and "The town was flooded," make it a compound sentence. Semicolons are used to join these clauses.
A complex sentence includes one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. Since this sentence does not have any dependent clauses, it does not qualify as complex.
A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. This sentence has no dependent clauses, so it cannot be classified as compound-complex.

questions.explanation: The sentence is a compound sentence because it has two independent clauses, “It had rained for days” and “The town was flooded.” These clauses are joined by semicolons.

questions.text: I waited for the bus, but it was late.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause. Since this sentence has two independent clauses, it cannot be classified as simple.
The coordinating conjunction "but" connects the two independent clauses in the sentence, "I waited for the bus" and "it was late," making it a compound sentence.
A complex sentence includes one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. This sentence does not have any dependent clauses, so it is not complex.
A compound-complex sentence contains at least two independent clauses and one or more dependent clauses. This sentence only has independent clauses, making it not compound-complex.

questions.explanation: The sentence has two independent clauses, "I waited for the bus" and "it was late," joined by the coordinating conjunction “but”, so it is a compound sentence.

questions.text: As the airplane climbed, I saw the ocean.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: Because it provides information in an uncomplicated manner, the sentence is declarative. It describes what transpired rather than making a request, giving an order, or expressing an intense feeling.
This option is incorrect because an imperative sentence issues a command or request. The given sentence does not instruct anyone to do anything.
This option is incorrect, as an optative sentence expresses a wish or desire. The example provided does not convey a wish but rather states an observation.
This option is incorrect because an exclamatory sentence conveys strong emotion or excitement. The provided sentence does not express any emotion but merely reports information.

questions.explanation: The sentence is declarative because it gives information in a straightforward way. It tells us what happened, and it does not express a wish, command, or sudden emotion.

questions.text: Please grant me a loan.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence states a fact or opinion and ends with a period. The given sentence does not state a fact but makes a request, which makes this option incorrect.
An imperative sentence requests something or issues an order. Because it makes a courteous request, the sentence is imperative.
An optative sentence expresses a wish or desire. The given sentence does not express a wish but rather requests an action, making this option incorrect.
An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion and usually ends with an exclamation mark. The sentence provided does not convey strong emotion, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: An imperative sentence gives a command or makes a request. The given sentence is an imperative sentence because it's making a polite request.

questions.text: All the best!
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A declarative sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion and ends with a period. For example, "The sky is blue". This option is incorrect because "All the best!' does not state a fact or opinion.
An imperative sentence gives a command or request. For instance, "Please close the door". This option is incorrect because "All the best!' is not commanding or requesting anything.

"All the best!" is an optative sentence since it conveys a wish for someone's prosperity or well-being. In a courteous manner, it expresses hope and goodwill. Because its primary goal is to convey a desire for the good of another person, it is an obvious example of an optative sentence.

An exclamatory sentence expresses strong emotion and ends with an exclamation mark, like 'What a beautiful day!'. This option is incorrect because "All the best!' does not express an intense feeling but rather a wish for good fortune.

questions.explanation: The sentence “All the best!” is an optative sentence because it expresses a wish for someone’s success or well-being. It conveys hope and goodwill in a polite tone. This makes it a clear example of an optative sentence, as its main purpose is to express a wish for another person’s benefit.

questions.text: God blesses everyone.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: This sentence makes a claim about what God has done. Declarative sentences are used to express facts or beliefs.
Imperative sentences issue commands or requests. The sentence does not instruct anyone to do something, so this option is incorrect.
Optative sentences express wishes or desires. While the phrase 'God blesses' might imply a wish, it is not structured as an optative, making this option incorrect.
Exclamatory sentences express strong emotions or exclamations. The sentence 'God blesses everyone' does not convey a strong emotion but rather a statement, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Declarative sentences are used to state facts or beliefs, and this sentence presents a statement about God's actions.

questions.text: Don’t move.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: An optative sentence expresses a wish or desire. The sentence 'Don't move' does not express a wish; thus, this option is incorrect.
The sentence is imperative because it issues a command.
A declarative sentence makes a statement or expresses an opinion. 'Don’t move' does not make a statement; this option is incorrect.
A subjunctive sentence expresses wishes, hypotheticals, or conditions contrary to fact. 'Don’t move' does not fit this definition, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The sentence gives a command, which makes it imperative.

questions.text: She forgot her friend's birthday, so she sent her a card when she finally remembered.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: A simple sentence contains only one independent clause and no dependent clauses. This sentence has multiple clauses.
A compound sentence consists of two or more independent clauses joined by a coordinating conjunction. This sentence also contains a dependent clause, so it is not just a compound sentence.
A complex sentence contains one independent clause and at least one dependent clause. While this sentence has a dependent clause, it also has two independent clauses, making it more complex.

"She sent her a card" and "She forgot her friend's birthday" are two separate sentences. "When she finally remembered" is another dependent clause. A compound-complex sentence consists of two independent clauses and one dependent clause.

questions.explanation: Two independent clauses: “She forgot her friend's birthday" and “She sent her a card.” There is also a dependent clause, “when she finally remembered”. A sentence with two independent clauses and a dependent clause is a compound-complex sentence.
